evals.name: product-list-module
evals.prompt: Create a VIPER module for a product list screen. The screen should fetch products from an API, show loading/error/empty states, display products in a table view, and let users tap a product to navigate to a detail screen. Use proper VIPER patterns with protocol contracts, module builder, and correct memory management.
evals.expected_output: A complete VIPER module with Contract (6 protocols), passive ViewController, UIKit-free Presenter with ViewState mapping, Interactor with injected service, Router with weak VC reference, and enum-based Builder that wires everything correctly.
assertions: Defines module contract with separate protocols for each VIPER boundary (at least ViewInput, PresenterInput, InteractorInput, InteractorOutput, RouterInput)
All protocols used for weak references are constrained to AnyObject
Presenter does NOT import UIKit — only Foundation
View (ViewController) is passive: forwards viewDidLoad to Presenter, renders ViewState, does not make content decisions
Interactor receives service dependency via constructor injection (protocol type, not concrete)
Interactor uses [weak self] in async callbacks
Presenter holds weak reference to View
Interactor holds weak reference to Presenter (output)
Router holds weak reference to ViewController
Uses ViewState enum (loading/loaded/empty/error) — not separate boolean flags
Presenter maps domain data to ViewModels before passing to View
Builder/Module factory creates all components and wires references before returning VC
Router navigates to detail screen by creating the destination module via its Builder
View protocol methods are at a higher abstraction than UIKit widgets — e.g. `display(_ viewModel:)` not `tableView.reloadData()` or `setLabelText(_:)`
Product entities are defined OUTSIDE the module folder — in a shared domain layer, not inside the module directory
Builder is enum-based (not a class) to prevent accidental instantiation
Router's viewController property is typed as `UIViewController?` (weak optional) — not the concrete ViewController subclass

evals.name: profile-refactor
evals.expected_output: A refactored VIPER module with: networking extracted to a service protocol injected into Interactor, business logic in Interactor, date/currency formatting in Presenter, passive VC that only renders and forwards events, Router handling all navigation, and a Builder wiring everything with correct weak references.
assertions: Creates a contract file with protocols for all VIPER boundaries
Presenter does NOT import UIKit
URLSession networking extracted to a separate service protocol (e.g. ProfileServiceProtocol) injected into Interactor
Interactor accesses userId without force-unwrapping — passed via constructor, configured property, or method parameter (not var userId: String!)
View only forwards user actions to Presenter (didTapSettings, didSelectPost, didTapChangeAvatar)
Presenter maps User entity to a display-ready ViewModel (formatted dates, etc.)
Router handles ALL navigation: push to settings, push to post detail, present image picker
Router does NOT create VCs with force-unwrapped or directly set properties — uses Builder pattern for destinations
Uses ViewState enum instead of separate isLoading/error/user booleans
All back-references are weak (Presenter→View, Interactor→Output, Router→VC)
Presenter decides which ViewState to show based on Interactor results
Presenter does NOT set `view?.title = user.name` or any UINavigationItem property directly — title is part of the ViewModel passed via the View protocol
User and Post entities defined outside the module folder in a shared domain layer

evals.name: presenter-tests
evals.prompt: Write comprehensive unit tests for a VIPER Presenter that handles an order checkout flow. The Presenter should: show loading when checkout starts, call the Interactor to validate the order, show error if validation fails, call the Interactor to process payment on validation success, navigate to confirmation on payment success, and show error on payment failure. Include tests for the full communication chain and memory leak detection.
evals.expected_output: Complete test suite with mock View, mock Interactor, mock Router. Tests cover: loading state shown, Interactor methods called, error mapping, navigation on success, and memory leak detection via weak reference assertions.
assertions: Creates mock objects for View, Interactor, and Router protocols with call tracking (callCount, receivedArgs)
Tests the full chain: user action → Presenter → Interactor call → Interactor output → Presenter → View update
Tests loading state is shown when checkout begins
Tests validation failure maps to user-friendly error message on View
Tests successful validation triggers payment processing on Interactor
Tests successful payment triggers Router navigation to confirmation
Tests payment failure shows error on View
Includes memory leak detection using weak reference and XCTAssertNil in addTeardownBlock
Uses setUp/tearDown or makeSUT factory to create and manage SUT and mocks lifecycle
Mock View protocol is constrained to AnyObject (since Presenter holds weak reference)
Mock Interactor call tracking uses named properties like `validateOrderCallCount` or `receivedOrderId` — not just booleans
Router test scenario creates a strong reference to ViewController in the test to prevent premature deallocation via Router's weak ref

evals.name: module-communication
evals.prompt:
I have an OrderListModule that shows a list of orders. When the user taps an order, it navigates to OrderDetailModule. In the detail screen, the user can delete the order. After deletion, the list should refresh. Also, the detail screen has a button to open an ItemPickerModule (presented modally) that lets the user add an item to the order — when an item is picked, both the detail and the list should update.

Implement the inter-module communication using VIPER delegate patterns. Show the protocols, how each module's Presenter/Router connects, and how data flows back from child to parent modules.
evals.expected_output: Three VIPER modules with delegate protocols for backward communication. OrderDetail reports deletion via delegate to OrderList. ItemPicker reports selection via delegate. Delegates are wired through Routers during module creation.
assertions: Defines a ModuleOutput or delegate protocol for OrderDetailModule that notifies parent of deletion
Defines a ModuleOutput or delegate protocol for ItemPickerModule that returns the selected item
Delegate protocols are AnyObject-constrained for weak references
OrderList Presenter (or its delegate adapter) conforms to OrderDetail's ModuleOutput
Delegate is passed through Router → Builder as a parameter when creating child modules
ItemPicker dismissal is handled by the PRESENTING module's Router, not by ItemPicker itself
OrderList refreshes data when notified of deletion or item addition via delegate callback
Child module Builder accepts delegate as a parameter — NOT set as a property after build() returns
OrderList Presenter conforms to the delegate protocol — NOT OrderList's Router

evals.name: code-review-antipatterns
evals.expected_output: Identifies at least 9 anti-patterns including the Router holding a Presenter reference. Provides corrected code with protocols, weak references, AnyObject constraints, navigation moved to Router, and business logic moved to Interactor.
assertions: Identifies that Presenter imports UIKit — fixes by removing UIKit import and using Foundation only
Identifies that all references use concrete types instead of protocols — introduces protocol contracts
Identifies strong back-references (Interactor→Presenter, Presenter→View) — fixes with weak references
Identifies business logic in Presenter (filtering, sorting) — moves to Interactor
Identifies synchronous blocking network call — replaces with async pattern
Identifies navigation happening directly in Presenter — moves to Router
Identifies Router holding strong reference to ViewController — fixes with weak
Identifies missing AnyObject constraints on protocols used for weak references
Identifies that DashboardRouter holds `var presenter: DashboardPresenter!` — Router should ONLY reference ViewController, never Presenter; this creates an additional retain cycle
Fixed Router implements the RouterInput protocol with actual navigation methods (e.g. `func navigateToItemDetail(item:)`) — not just an empty class with properties
All implicitly-unwrapped optionals (`var x: Type!`) on back-references converted to `weak var x: ProtocolType?`

evals.name: async-await-integration
evals.prompt:
Build a VIPER module for a chat feature that uses async/await and AsyncStream. The module should:
1. Fetch initial messages from an API on viewDidLoad
2. Subscribe to a WebSocket-like message stream (AsyncStream) for real-time updates
3. Cancel the stream subscription when the module is dismissed
4. Handle errors from both the initial fetch and the stream
5. Use @MainActor where appropriate

Show how async/await changes the traditional VIPER callback pattern (specifically: does the Interactor still need an Output protocol?).
evals.expected_output: A VIPER module where async/await replaces the InteractorOutput callback protocol. Presenter is @MainActor, Interactor methods return values directly via async throws. AsyncStream for real-time messages with proper Task cancellation in deinit.
assertions: Presenter is annotated with @MainActor
Interactor fetch method uses async throws return type — Presenter awaits results directly (e.g. `let messages = try await interactor.fetchMessages()`) rather than receiving them via a callback
Uses AsyncStream or AsyncSequence for the real-time message subscription
Task is stored as a named property (e.g. `private var streamTask: Task<Void, Never>?`) and explicitly cancelled with `.cancel()` in deinit or viewWillDisappear to prevent leaks
Stream consumption loop includes `try Task.checkCancellation()` OR `guard !Task.isCancelled else { break }` before processing each message
Interactor does NOT have @MainActor — business logic runs off main thread
Error handling covers both initial fetch failure and stream failure separately
Presenter awaits Interactor methods directly — when async/await is used throughout, a callback-based InteractorOutput protocol is not needed
When the Task is cancelled, the AsyncStream producer is also cleaned up (e.g. via onTermination handler on the stream continuation)

evals.name: retain-cycle-detection
evals.expected_output: Identifies 3 retain cycles: (1) View→Presenter→View (both strong), (2) Presenter→Interactor→Presenter (both strong), (3) Router→VC (strong). Protocols missing AnyObject constraint prevents using weak. Fixes all with weak references and AnyObject constraints.
assertions: Identifies that Presenter→View is strong (var view: SettingsViewProtocol!) and should be weak
Identifies that Interactor→Presenter is strong (var presenter: SettingsInteractorOutputProtocol!) and should be weak
Identifies that Router→VC is strong (var viewController: SettingsViewController!) and should be weak
Identifies that protocols LACK AnyObject constraint — this is the ROOT CAUSE preventing weak references (not just a side effect)
Adds AnyObject constraint to SettingsViewProtocol and SettingsInteractorOutputProtocol
Changes Presenter's view reference to weak var
Changes Interactor's presenter reference to weak var
Changes Router's viewController reference to weak var
Adds deinit with print/logging to ALL VIPER components (View, Presenter, Interactor, Router) — not just two

Explains that after fixes, ALL three cycles are broken: View owns Presenter (strong unchanged), but Presenter→View is weak; Presenter owns Interactor (strong unchanged), but Interactor→Presenter is weak; Router→VC is weak

evals.name: swiftui-migration
evals.prompt:
I have an existing VIPER module (OrderListModule) with a UIKit ViewController, Presenter, Interactor, Router, and Builder. I want to replace ONLY the View layer with SwiftUI while keeping everything else (Presenter, Interactor, Router, Builder) unchanged. The existing Presenter conforms to OrderListPresenterInput and calls OrderListViewInput methods on the View.

Show me how to create a SwiftUI View + ObservableObject adapter that bridges the VIPER ViewInput protocol to SwiftUI's reactive system. The Router should still use UINavigationController for navigation (push/present).
evals.expected_output: An ObservableObject adapter conforming to OrderListViewInput that bridges VIPER callbacks to @Published properties. A SwiftUI View using @ObservedObject. UIHostingController wraps the SwiftUI view. Router and Builder updated to use UIHostingController. No NavigationLink — UIKit navigation only.
assertions: Creates an ObservableObject adapter class that conforms to the VIPER ViewInput protocol
Adapter uses @Published properties to drive SwiftUI View reactivity
SwiftUI View references the adapter via @ObservedObject or @StateObject
UIHostingController wraps the SwiftUI View for UIKit navigation compatibility
Builder/Module factory updated to create UIHostingController instead of UIViewController subclass
Router's weak viewController reference updated to handle UIHostingController
Does NOT use NavigationLink or NavigationStack inside the SwiftUI View — navigation stays in Router via UIKit
Existing Interactor code requires ZERO changes for the migration
Presenter's `view` property type remains the VIPER ViewInput protocol (e.g. `weak var view: OrderListViewInput?`) — NOT UIHostingController or the ObservableObject adapter class directly
ObservableObject adapter ensures main-thread safety — either via @MainActor annotation on the class OR by wrapping @Published mutations in DispatchQueue.main.async
Presenter requires ZERO changes — it continues to call `view?.display(viewModel:)` as before; only the adapter class is new

evals.name: complex-navigation
evals.prompt:
Build VIPER Router patterns for these three navigation scenarios in a single app:

1. A TabBarController with 3 tabs, each tab having its own NavigationController and VIPER module
2. Tab 1 (Feed) pushes to a detail screen, which presents a modal comment composer. After posting a comment, both the detail and feed list should refresh.
3. Tab 2 (Search) has a multi-step filter flow: SearchResults → FilterStep1 → FilterStep2 → FilterStep3, where the user can go back to any step. When filters are applied, SearchResults refreshes.

Show the Router code, how each Router references its VC, and how the filter results propagate back.
evals.expected_output: Multiple Router implementations handling TabBar, push, modal, and multi-step flows. Delegate protocols for backward communication. Each Router holds weak VC reference. Filter state passed forward and results communicated back via delegate chain.
assertions: Each tab's VIPER module has its own Router with a weak reference to its ViewController
TabBar setup does NOT live inside a single VIPER module — it's a separate coordinator, AppDelegate, or SceneDelegate factory
Each tab's NavigationController is created OUTSIDE any VIPER module — by the app-level setup code
Modal comment composer dismissal is handled by the PRESENTING Router (Feed Detail's Router), not by the composer itself
Comment posting triggers a delegate callback that reaches both FeedDetail and FeedList for refresh
Filter flow uses forward data passing (filter criteria passed through Router → Builder → Presenter)
Filter results propagate back to SearchResults via delegate protocol — NOT NotificationCenter or shared singleton
All Router→ViewController references are weak
FeedDetail Router (the presenting Router) calls `viewController?.dismiss(animated: true)` AFTER receiving delegate callback — the CommentComposer module never calls dismiss on itself

evals.name: interactor-tests
evals.prompt:
Write unit tests for a VIPER Interactor that handles a shopping cart. The CartInteractor should:
1. Add items to cart (with quantity validation — max 10 per item)
2. Remove items from cart
3. Calculate total price (subtotal + tax at 8.875%)
4. Apply discount codes (valid codes give 10% off, invalid codes produce an error)
5. Persist cart state via an injected CartStorageProtocol

The Interactor depends on CartStorageProtocol and DiscountServiceProtocol, both injected via constructor. Write tests with proper mocks for all dependencies.
evals.expected_output: Test suite with mock CartStorage and mock DiscountService. Tests cover: add/remove items, quantity limits, price calculation, discount code application (valid/invalid), and persistence calls. All dependencies mocked via protocols.
assertions: Creates mock implementations for CartStorageProtocol and DiscountServiceProtocol with call tracking
Tests that adding an item increases the cart item count
Tests quantity validation rejects adding more than 10 of the same item
Tests removing an item decreases cart count
Tests total calculation includes subtotal + tax (8.875%)
Tests valid discount code applies 10% reduction to total
Tests invalid discount code produces an error via the Interactor's output
Interactor output (presenter mock) verifies correct callbacks for success and error cases
Interactor is instantiated directly with mock dependencies via constructor injection — NOT via Builder or Module.build()
Tax calculation test verifies correct tax amount (8.875%) — either via XCTAssertEqual with accuracy: for Double, or exact comparison for Decimal types
Tests verify CartStorage.save() is called with the correct updated cart state after adding/removing items
Presenter mock is held weakly — test includes memory leak detection via addTeardownBlock { [weak mockPresenter] in XCTAssertNil(mockPresenter) }

evals.name: builder-assembly-tabbar
evals.prompt: Create a VIPER module for a HomeViewController that will be the root of the first tab in a UITabBarController. The Presenter needs to call `view?.showWelcomeMessage(username:)` as part of the initial setup when the module loads. Write the Builder code. What timing issue can occur with UITabBarController, and how does your Builder avoid it?
evals.expected_output: Builder wires all VIPER references before returning VC. Identifies that UITabBarController calls viewDidLoad on tab children before their init finishes, causing Presenter to be nil when forwarded. Solves by wiring Presenter in Builder before returning VC, not relying on viewDidLoad.
assertions: Identifies that UITabBarController calls viewDidLoad on tab children before their init completes — meaning if Presenter is set in viewDidLoad, it may be nil when the VC first appears as a tab
Builder wires ALL references (Presenter→View, Interactor→Output, Router→VC) AND calls the initial setup before returning ViewController
Builder is enum-based with a static build() method — not a class that gets instantiated
Builder's build() method accepts external dependencies as parameters — not stored as Builder properties
ViewController does NOT assign `self.presenter` in its own viewDidLoad — the Builder does it before returning
Router's viewController property is typed as `UIViewController?` — not the concrete HomeViewController subclass

evals.name: thread-safety-dispatching
evals.prompt: Show the correct threading pattern for a VIPER module where the Interactor makes URLSession network calls on a background thread. Where exactly should DispatchQueue.main.async (or @MainActor) be placed in the VIPER chain to guarantee that all View updates are thread-safe? Show a concrete example with the correct placement, and explain why other placements are wrong.
evals.expected_output: DispatchQueue.main.async at the Interactor output boundary (before calling Presenter callback methods), OR Presenter is @MainActor and Interactor uses await MainActor.run {}. Explains Presenter must never call DispatchQueue.main.async — it assumes it's on main thread.
assertions: DispatchQueue.main.async or @MainActor is applied at the Interactor OUTPUT boundary — just before calling Presenter callback methods (e.g. `self?.presenter?.didLoad(data)`) — not inside the Presenter before calling View
Presenter does NOT contain DispatchQueue.main.async calls — Presenter assumes it's already on the main thread
Explains that placing DispatchQueue.main.async in Presenter (before calling view methods) is wrong because Interactor callbacks may themselves do work that assumes main thread
Alternative: if Presenter is marked @MainActor, Interactor can use `Task { @MainActor in await presenter?.didLoad(data) }` — this is equivalent and acceptable
View protocol callback methods are always called on main thread — this is enforced at ONE consistent boundary in the chain, not scattered throughout

evals.name: viewstate-enum-design
evals.prompt: Implement a VIPER Presenter for a social feed with these states: initial loading → content loaded (possibly empty) → loading more items (pagination, showing existing data) → error with retry → pull-to-refresh (loading fresh data). Show the ViewState enum definition and the Presenter's state transition logic. The user can trigger pull-to-refresh at any time, including while paginating.
evals.expected_output: ViewState enum with associated values covering all states. Presenter transitions between states cleanly. No separate boolean flags. Pull-to-refresh while paginating goes back to .loading. ViewState passed to View as a single unit.
assertions: ViewState is a Swift enum with associated values — not a struct or class with multiple boolean/optional fields
Defines at least these states: loading, loaded([FeedItem]), loadingMore([FeedItem]), error(String) — empty state can be a separate case OR represented as loaded([])
Pull-to-refresh while in loadingMore state is handled gracefully — either transitions to .loading (fresh start) or keeps showing existing content while refreshing (better UX)
Presenter calls a SINGLE view method like `view?.render(.loading)` — NOT separate calls like `view?.showLoading()` AND `view?.hideContent()`
Interactor never receives a ViewState — it only receives domain commands (fetchFeed, loadMoreFeed, refreshFeed)
View renders each enum case distinctly — table visibility, spinner, error UI driven by the single ViewState value

evals.name: god-interactor-decomposition
evals.expected_output: Split into separate use-case Interactors (ProfileLoadInteractor, AvatarUploadInteractor, etc.), each with a single use case. Services injected per Interactor. Presenter holds multiple Interactor references. No orchestrator Interactor.
assertions: Splits into at least 3 separate Interactors, each representing a single use case
Each Interactor has a focused public API matching its use case — a single method, or a small cohesive set of related methods (e.g. follow/unfollow as one use case)
Shared services (UserService, MediaService) are injected into each Interactor separately via constructor — not shared via singleton or property
No orchestrating Interactor that calls other Interactors internally — each Interactor talks directly to its required services
Presenter holds multiple Interactor references (one per use case) — this is correct VIPER, not an anti-pattern
Each Interactor has its own output protocol — not a single massive ProfileInteractorOutput with methods for all use cases
Presenter conforms to all relevant output protocols (or delegates to separate handlers)

evals.name: entities-domain-layer
evals.prompt: Design the Entity and domain data layer for a VIPER e-commerce app with four modules: ProductListModule, ProductDetailModule, CartModule, and CheckoutModule. Show where entity files should live in the folder structure, what they contain, and how Interactors pass data to Presenters without exposing raw entities.
evals.expected_output: Entities in a shared domain layer outside module folders. Interactors map Entities to response structs before passing to Presenters. Entities are plain structs. Multiple modules reference the same Product entity type.
assertions: Entities (Product, CartItem, Order) are defined in a SHARED domain layer OUTSIDE individual module folders — not inside any single module's directory
Entity files contain pure Swift structs with no UIKit imports — only Foundation (for Codable, Hashable, etc.)
Interactors do NOT pass raw Entity types to Presenter output methods — they map to intermediate result/response structs first
Different modules reference the SAME shared Product entity type — NOT separate types like ProductListItem vs ProductDetailProduct for the same concept
Presenter maps response structs to ViewModels before passing to View — entities never reach the View layer
Computed properties in entities contain derived values only — no network calls, no formatting, no business logic

evals.name: error-propagation-chain
evals.prompt: Implement the error propagation chain for a VIPER checkout flow. A NetworkService throws URLError, the CartService wraps it in a CartError, the Interactor receives CartError and notifies the Presenter, and the Presenter maps it to a user-facing message on the View. Show the complete chain from URLError at the service level to the error string displayed to the user.
evals.expected_output: Layered error transformation: URLError → CartError (service boundary) → Interactor maps CartError to domain result → Presenter maps domain result to user message string. Each layer transforms the error to match the abstraction of the next layer. View only sees strings.
assertions: Error type is transformed at each VIPER boundary — URLError/NSError never reaches the Presenter
Service layer maps URLError to a domain-level CartError enum with meaningful cases
Interactor calls Presenter output with a result type or specific error case — NOT the raw CartError or URLError
Presenter maps domain error cases to human-readable strings — it does NOT pass Error or NSError objects to the View
View protocol method receives a String error message — NOT an Error type: `func showError(_ message: String)` NOT `func showError(_ error: Error)`
Error cases are exhaustively handled — no wildcard catch-all that swallows specific error context
